summary: Nadia Asparouhova reflects on her four-plus years of independent research into open source software economics, describing how she unintentionally constructed her own version of a PhD program. She outlines three phases: discovery (funded by Ford Foundation grants, building relationships and publishing a foundational report), testing (working at GitHub to validate hypotheses through hands-on product experience), and refining (synthesizing everything into a book at Protocol Labs). She argues that independent research can be a viable alternative to a traditional PhD, particularly when the researcher's goal is to influence public discourse and reach practitioners rather than academics. The essay provides practical advice for aspiring independent researchers, including working in public, building support networks, and creating artifacts suited to your audience rather than conforming to academic norms.
key_points: Independent research can replicate the value of a PhD when structured with intentional phases: discovery, hypothesis testing through hands-on experience, and synthesis into a substantial artifact
Being an outsider to open source was an advantage rather than a liability, enabling pattern recognition across projects that insiders embedded in one ecosystem couldn't achieve
The combination of theoretical research and practical hands-on experience (working at GitHub) was far more powerful than either alone — rhetoric had to meet reality
Funding for independent research is inherently gig-based and uncertain, but writing thoughtfully in public about niche topics reliably attracts sponsors and opportunities
The isolation of long-form synthesis work is the most emotionally grueling phase of research, and building accountability structures and support networks is essential to survive it
money_quotes: Most open source developers only know their own experience, which is often limited to one project or one language ecosystem. But as an outsider, I could look for patterns across lots of different projects.
Knowing that someone cares about what you're doing is one of those things that seems totally trivial unless you're currently in that flailing-about sort of situation.
I was forced to make rhetoric meet reality.
Don't just research for the sake of research, or soon you'll just feel like a person who reads all day (which might sound cool, but I promise is actually soul-crushing).
If I want to cite a developer's tweet or Taylor Swift lyrics instead of an academic paper, I can just do that.
You're hopping from place to place, living in mortal fear of the day that some CFO type starts to run the numbers and wonder what the heck you're doing there.
When I think about alternatives to a PhD, I imagine combining the best of both worlds: injecting theoretical concepts directly into the veins of practitioners.
Independent research affords an incredible amount of freedom, but getting a blank check can be surprisingly scary.
key_insight: A self-directed research career that cycles between theoretical exploration and hands-on practical experience can rival a traditional PhD in rigor and exceed it in real-world impact, provided the researcher builds accountability structures, works in public, and stays close to their subject matter.
